title: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
code: public bool Union(int x, int y)

parent.label: parent[]  (index -> parent; root points at itself)

frames.caption: Initialize: parent = [0,1,2,3,4] — every element is its own root, so 5 singleton sets.
frames.note: Union-Find starts as n singleton sets: parent[i] = i means i is its own root, so there are n separate components. The parent array IS the forest — there are no node objects, just an index-to-parent map where a root is the one index that points at itself. Everything below is two operations over this one array: Find walks to a root, Union links one root under another.

frames.caption: Union(0, 1): find the root of each endpoint.
frames.note: Process edges one at a time. Union first resolves both endpoints to their set roots; whether they already match decides if this edge merges two sets or closes a cycle. This incremental "add an edge, are they connected now?" pattern is exactly what Union-Find is for.

frames.caption: Roots differ: attach smaller root 1 under larger root 0; parent[1] = 0. Count-- -> 4.
frames.note: The two roots differ, so the elements were in different sets — a real merge. Union by size attaches the SMALLER tree under the LARGER root (swap rx/ry if needed) so the combined tree stays shallow; that bounds tree height and is the other half of the near-O(1) guarantee. We repoint the loser root at the winner and add its size in. One real union means one fewer component, so Count--.

frames.caption: Union(1, 2): find the root of each endpoint.
frames.note: Process edges one at a time. Union first resolves both endpoints to their set roots; whether they already match decides if this edge merges two sets or closes a cycle. This incremental "add an edge, are they connected now?" pattern is exactly what Union-Find is for.

frames.caption: Find(rx): 1 -> parent 0; path-halving re-points 1 at 0, then climb.
frames.note: Find walks parent pointers up to the root — the set representative. Two elements are in the same set IFF they reach the same root. The path-halving line parent[x] = parent[parent[x]] re-points each visited node at its grandparent as we climb, flattening the tree a little on every lookup so future Finds are shorter. This compression is half of why the structure is near-O(1).

frames.caption: Roots differ: attach smaller root 2 under larger root 0; parent[2] = 0. Count-- -> 3.
frames.note: The two roots differ, so the elements were in different sets — a real merge. Union by size attaches the SMALLER tree under the LARGER root (swap rx/ry if needed) so the combined tree stays shallow; that bounds tree height and is the other half of the near-O(1) guarantee. We repoint the loser root at the winner and add its size in. One real union means one fewer component, so Count--.

frames.caption: Union(3, 4): find the root of each endpoint.
frames.note: Process edges one at a time. Union first resolves both endpoints to their set roots; whether they already match decides if this edge merges two sets or closes a cycle. This incremental "add an edge, are they connected now?" pattern is exactly what Union-Find is for.

frames.caption: Roots differ: attach smaller root 4 under larger root 3; parent[4] = 3. Count-- -> 2.
frames.note: The two roots differ, so the elements were in different sets — a real merge. Union by size attaches the SMALLER tree under the LARGER root (swap rx/ry if needed) so the combined tree stays shallow; that bounds tree height and is the other half of the near-O(1) guarantee. We repoint the loser root at the winner and add its size in. One real union means one fewer component, so Count--.

frames.caption: Union(2, 4): find the root of each endpoint.
frames.note: Process edges one at a time. Union first resolves both endpoints to their set roots; whether they already match decides if this edge merges two sets or closes a cycle. This incremental "add an edge, are they connected now?" pattern is exactly what Union-Find is for.

frames.caption: Find(rx): 2 -> parent 0; path-halving re-points 2 at 0, then climb.
frames.note: Find walks parent pointers up to the root — the set representative. Two elements are in the same set IFF they reach the same root. The path-halving line parent[x] = parent[parent[x]] re-points each visited node at its grandparent as we climb, flattening the tree a little on every lookup so future Finds are shorter. This compression is half of why the structure is near-O(1).

frames.caption: Find(ry): 4 -> parent 3; path-halving re-points 4 at 3, then climb.
frames.note: Find walks parent pointers up to the root — the set representative. Two elements are in the same set IFF they reach the same root. The path-halving line parent[x] = parent[parent[x]] re-points each visited node at its grandparent as we climb, flattening the tree a little on every lookup so future Finds are shorter. This compression is half of why the structure is near-O(1).

frames.caption: Roots differ: attach smaller root 3 under larger root 0; parent[3] = 0. Count-- -> 1.
frames.note: The two roots differ, so the elements were in different sets — a real merge. Union by size attaches the SMALLER tree under the LARGER root (swap rx/ry if needed) so the combined tree stays shallow; that bounds tree height and is the other half of the near-O(1) guarantee. We repoint the loser root at the winner and add its size in. One real union means one fewer component, so Count--.

frames.caption: Done. parent = [0, 0, 0, 0, 3]; 1 connected component: {0,1,2,3,4}.
frames.note: All edges processed. With both optimizations — path halving in Find and union by size in Union — each operation is O(alpha(n)) amortized, where alpha is the inverse Ackermann function (<= 4 for any realistic n). That is "near constant," not literally O(1) and not O(log n). The final Count is the number of connected components (LC 323).
